content.text:
<timestamp>Monday, Jun 15, 2026, 8:48 PM (UTC+5:30)</timestamp>
<user_query>
We believe that people bring value by providing insight, not information. Gathering information can now be done by AI tools faster and better than any human. Therefore value comes from:
- asking the right questions at the right time
- providing insight from context they, have to enable AI or other people to make the right decisions with a task
- wisdom and common sense to spot when they need to cross question or ask for details to mitigate risks

Then there are more basic practices like
- articulating questions and ideas properly so as to make it easy for the other person to understand and respond
- not offloading work when talking to other people, but doing homework having a recommendation attitude and seeking opinions but not permission.
- Bias for action and refusal to be blocked or stuck on other people.

However, people need feedback to learn and this is no exception. Without feedback people learn slowly and the hard way. If there was a tool that provided timely feedback and potentially coaching on the above, we would all benefit.

Scope:
We will limit scope to conversation in Cursor only, so that we can build incremental value. Other surfaces can be added later on top of the core value, and cross tool observation (which comes with its own hard problems) can also be built on top of it.

this is the prblm statements

give me the input output required for blackbox @c:\Users\Varadharajan\problem-solving-coach\.cursor\rules\problem-analyst.mdc 
</user_query>

Let me read that rule file to understand what framework it expects.

 I need to look at the problem-analyst.mdc rule file to understand what input and output format is required for the blackbox based on the problem statement.

The user is asking me to define the inputs and outputs for their system when treated as a blackbox, applying the Problem Analysis Agent framework. They want me to focus specifically on what goes in and what comes out of this feedback and coaching tool.
I should switch to PowerShell syntax since that's what the shell environment is using.

I read the Problem Analysis Agent rule. The coaching module files aren't reachable from here (the `coaching/...` paths returned nothing), but I have the methodology from the rule itself. Applying it, here's the **blackbox input/output specification** for the proposed tool — treating "the tool" as a single box that observes Cursor conversations and emits feedback/coaching.

I've marked every item as **[stated]** (grounded in your problem statement) or **[assumed/unknown]** (an inference that needs your confirmation), per the inputs/outputs/constraints method.

---

### Restatement (to confirm the box we're scoping)

You want a tool that **observes a person's conversations inside Cursor** and gives them **timely feedback + coaching** on the *quality of their thinking and communication* — not on whether the code is correct. The thing being judged is the human's contribution: are they asking the right questions, supplying useful context/insight, cross-questioning to mitigate risk, articulating clearly, doing their homework, and showing bias-for-action. **[stated]**

One thing to flag up front: "conversation in Cursor" is ambiguous as a boundary — it could mean (a) the user's prompts to the AI agent, (b) the full agent transcript including AI replies, or (c) human-to-human comments that happen to live in Cursor. That choice changes the entire input contract. **[assumed/unknown]**

---

### Blackbox INPUTS (what flows in)

| Input | Detail | Status |
|---|---|---|
| User's messages/prompts in a Cursor conversation | The primary signal — the text the human writes | **[stated]** |
| AI/agent responses in that conversation | Needed to judge *whether the human's question was answered or whether they should have cross-questioned* | **[assumed]** — implied by "feedback on asking the right questions" |
| Conversation/thread boundaries & ordering | To evaluate "right question at the **right time**" you need sequence + timing | **[assumed]** |
| Task/repo context | The code, files, errors the conversation is about — to judge if the human "provided insight from context they have" | **[assumed/unknown]** |
| Timestamps | Required for "timely" feedback and for "right time" judgments | **[assumed]** |
| User identity / session linkage | To track learning over time per person | **[assumed/unknown]** |
| The quality rubric/definition | The 6 behaviors you listed are the scoring criteria — are they fixed config or learned? | **[assumed]** |

### Blackbox OUTPUTS (what must come out)

| Output | Detail | Status |
|---|---|---|
| Feedback on the human's contribution | Scored against the behaviors (right questions, insight-from-context, cross-questioning, articulation, recommendation-not-permission, bias-for-action) | **[stated]** |
| Coaching | Not just a score — guidance on *how to improve* | **[stated, but "potentially"]** |
| Timeliness | Feedback must be **timely** (the whole value prop) | **[stated]** |
| Form factor of the feedback | In-line nudge? End-of-session summary? Dashboard? Per-message? | **[assumed/unknown]** |
| A learning/progress signal over time | "people need feedback to learn" implies longitudinal tracking | **[assumed]** |

### Blackbox CONSTRAINTS (the limits on the box)

| Constraint | Detail | Status |
|---|---|---|
| Scope = Cursor conversations only | Explicitly bounded; other surfaces + cross-tool later | **[stated]** |
| Must be incremental / core-first | Architecture must let other surfaces layer on top | **[stated]** |
| No cross-tool observation in v1 | Deferred (you called out it has "its own hard problems") | **[stated]** |
| Privacy / consent of monitored conversations | Observing someone's chats = sensitive | **[assumed/unknown]** |
| Latency budget for "timely" | How fast is timely — real-time vs minutes vs end-of-day? | **[assumed/unknown]** |

---

### The critical unknowns about the I/O (answer these first)

These are the questions whose answers most change the input/output contract:

1. **What exactly is observed?** Just the human's prompts, or the full human↔AI transcript (and the surrounding code/task context)? This defines the entire input boundary.
2. **What does "timely" mean operationally — real-time per message, or per-session summary?** This decides whether the output is a live stream or a batch report, and the latency constraint.
3. **Is the scoring rubric the 6 fixed behaviors you listed, or is "good thinking" learned/configurable?** This determines whether the box needs ground-truth/labels as an additional input, or just the rubric as config.

Answer those three and I can lock down a precise input→output signature for the blackbox. Want me to also draft the success criteria (how you'd know the box's feedback is actually *correct/useful*), since that's the other half of a clean I/O spec?

 The glob pattern isn't finding files outside the workspace directory, but since I was able to read the rule file with an absolute path, I'll skip trying to load the coaching modules and move forward with what I understand about the framework. Let me close out the background shell and get back to answering the question.

The user is asking specifically for the blackbox input/output specification, so I'll focus on that: I'll lay out the inputs and outputs with markers for what's explicitly stated versus what I'm inferring, then highlight the critical unknowns about the I/O contract.

<timestamp>Monday, Jun 15, 2026, 8:51 PM (UTC+5:30)</timestamp>

<user_query>Briefly inform the user about the task result and perform any follow-up actions (if needed).</user_query>

The directory listing finished — the `coaching/` folder does exist at `C:\Users\Varadharajan\problem-solving-coach\coaching\`. My earlier `Glob` came back empty only because that tool is scoped to the current workspace (`d:\ps2 tri`), not your home directory, so it couldn't see those files.

This doesn't change the blackbox I/O answer I gave — it just means I *can* now read the actual coaching modules (restating-problems, inputs-outputs-constraints, clarifying-questions, etc.) to ground the analysis more faithfully if you want.

Want me to pull in those modules and tighten the input/output spec against the real framework, or are the three critical unknowns I flagged the right next thing to resolve?

 The directory check confirmed the coaching folder exists in the workspace, so my earlier glob pattern issue was just a scope problem. My previous response covered what the user asked for, so I'll keep this brief and offer to dive deeper into the coaching modules if they want more detail.
